---
name: local test env
description: Create a Local Test Environemnt
---

<!-- Tip: Use /create-prompt in chat to generate content with agent assistance -->

I want to create a plan to be saved in a file, the name of your choosing, that will outline the steps to create a local test environment for our project.

The plan should include the necessary tools, configurations, and best practices to ensure a smooth setup process. Please provide a detailed outline that covers the following aspects:
- Local deployment in containers, docker in windows and podman in linux
- Database setup and configuration for the local environment. I understand that DML operations are performed in code, which is far from ideal and it will be changed in the future. Not now. And in the case of the local test environment I want the system to behave the exact same way it is todya. So I want the local test environment to use the same DML operations in code, and the same database as the production environment, which is NEON. However if it is not possible to use NEON in a local environment, we can use a Postgres container instead, but we should make sure that the system behaves the same way as it does with NEON, and that we can easily switch to NEON when we are ready to do so.
- Environment variable management, that probably needs to be added as additional code and configuration, we should avoid changing existing files, if we can implement that as module, greatly better, but if not we can add new files and configuration to manage environment variables in a way that is consistent with the existing codebase and does not require changing existing files.
- Containers should have troubleshooting tools and logging configured during image creation to allow for easy debugging and monitoring of the local test environment. This includes tools like `curl`, `ping`, `net-tools`, and logging configurations that capture relevant information for troubleshooting purposes.
- it should be one command, probably compose up, to start the environment although a script deciding what parameters to pass to compose is also OK, but the command should be simple and easy to remember, and it should start all the necessary containers and services for the local test environment.
- Whatever cannot be used for real (NEON database?) should be replaced (Postgres Container?)
- If not possible at all to use the APIs calls, such as postcode.io, they should be mocked or stubbed in a way that allows for testing the rest of the system without it. Even when possible I want the ability to allow mocking them as local containers instead of the real API, for testing purposes.
- Whenever the provider provides a test environment RESEND? STRIPE? CLAUDE? we should use them
- I really need to be able to test emails sending and receiving in the local environment, so I want to include a tool like MailHog or similar to capture and view emails sent from the application IF AND ONLY IF RESEND do not allow me to do that in my local environment.
- Ideally the system should support pluging in different AI agents instead of claude, I would like to make the local test environment as flexible as possible to allow for testing with different AI models and agents and costs, however if that imply changes for now I do not want to do it. And we should have necessarely a MOCKED version of the AI agent, that can be used for testing purposes without incurring costs or relying on external services, and that can be easily switched with the real AI agent when needed if possible via configuration or environemnt varriables, but if not we can implement it as a separate module that can be easily swapped with the real AI agent module when needed.
- I want a consistent plan to implement this. If multiple steps, multiple branches. We never update main branch. Create new branch for each step and sfor substeps just commit, one coimmit per functionnality. A Branch can have multiple commits, however I donot expect many 
- For this implementation We can Add files, configuration and code. We cannot remove  any existing code we cannot change code as well. We can only add new code and files, and configuration. This is to ensure that we do not break anything in the existing codebase while implementing the local test environment.
- Local test environment should be all the way defaulted to DEBUGGING at the most detailed level, to allow for easy troubleshooting and debugging during development. This includes setting appropriate logging levels, enabling verbose output, and configuring any necessary debugging tools or configurations to capture detailed information about the system's behavior in the local test environment, however we should allow for easy parametrization to move tha tto diffrent levels of logging such as info or warning, for when we want to test the system with less verbose logging.
- I am happy if the containers share the same 'machine' or 'hostname', they should have porper names and adresses asa well as ports be clearly specified and documented to avoid confusion and ensure that the different services can communicate with each other properly in the local test environment.
- Test credentials should be properly and securely managed, we cannot have hard coded code or even configuration files with plan text passwords, keys or tokens that have access to real services or data. We should use environment variables, secrets management tools, or other secure methods to manage test credentials in the local test environment, and ensure that they are not exposed in the codebase or configuration files.
- Another point that needs investigation before any porposal is tha tif we need the database to be pre-populated with some data for testing purposes, and if so, how we can achieve that in a way that is consistent with the existing codebase and does not require changing existing files. We should consider using migration scripts, seed files, or other methods to populate the database with test data in the local test environment, while ensuring that it does not interfere with the production database or require changes to existing code,. howver I understand that is reflected somehow in some sort of refacotirng that SHOULD NOT HAPPEN now.
- In the case any of the above conflicts with the instruciton of NOT CHANGING EXUISTING CODE AT ALL, then you should me warn in a separated section of the plan, preferrebly at the start.
- I want you to propose some metrics for measuring the success of the implementation of this plan. These metrics should be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) to help us evaluate the effectiveness of the local test environment and identify areas for improvement. Examples of metrics could include:
- Time taken to set up the local test environment from scratch.
- Number of successful test runs in the local test environment.
- Number of issues or bugs identified and resolved in the local test environment.
- Feedback from developers on the ease of use and effectiveness of the local test environment.
- Percentage of test cases that can be successfully executed in the local test environment compared to the production environment.
- Time taken to switch between different AI agents or models in the local test environment.
- Number of times the local test environment is used for testing and development purposes.  
Please provide a detailed plan that outlines the steps to create the local test environment, including any necessary tools, configurations, and best practices to ensure a smooth setup process. The plan should be organized in a clear and logical manner, with each step clearly defined and actionable. Additionally, please include any potential challenges or considerations that may arise during the implementation of the local test environment, and propose solutions or workarounds to address them.
